data.title: U.S. tariffs on Canada dairy, lumber may be on the way, Trump says
data.description:
Canada may be hit with reciprocal tariffs on its dairy and lumber products as soon as Friday, U.S. President Donald Trump said. 

Speaking in the Oval Office, Trump said U.S. dairy products carry a 250% export levy into Canada, and that tariffs are high on U.S. lumber. 

“They make it impossible for us to sell lumber or dairy products into Canada, but our numbers are a tiny fraction of that, almost nonexistent,” Trump said. “They'll be met with the exact same tariff unless they drop it, and that's what reciprocal means,” he said. “And we may do it as early as today,” or on Monday or Tuesday, he said. 

The Trump administration has been teeing up an April 2 announcement on reciprocal, or tit-for-tat, tariffs for U.S. trading partners. Softwood lumber has been an area of trade contention between the U.S. and Canada for years. 

Trump’s remarks Friday come after his decisions this week to, for one month, halt tariffs on some goods under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA, trade deal, including cars and auto parts. 

In markets Friday, lumber futures (LB1:COM) rose 1% to $639 per thousand board feet, coming off session lows in late-morning trade. The iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (WOOD) rose 0.5%, also coming off a weaker price. 

U.S. stocks (SP500)(VOO)(COMP:IND)(DJI) on Friday seesawed and faced weekly losses. Canada's TSX Index (SPTSX) gained 0.5%. 

U.S. lumber futures (LB1:COM) early this week hit 2022 highs after Trump told the Commerce Department to investigate potential harm to U.S. national security posed by lumber imports. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump plans to restrict student loan forgiveness in non-profit jobs - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump plans to rescind student loan forgiveness for borrowers who work for non-governmental organizations that engage in "improper activities" tied to such things as illegal immigration and terrorist groups, according to a media report.

White House Staff Secretary Will Scharf told Bloomberg News that Trump expects to sign an executive order to direct the Department of Education and the Treasury Department to modify the program to rescind eligibility.

In 2007, the government created a program to full forgive student loans for borrowers in public service jobs, such as teaching, nursing and public safety, if they made payments on the loans for at least 10 years and stayed in the profession.

However, before President Joe Biden took office only 7,000 public service workers received any form of debt cancellation through the program, Bloomberg said.

data.title: Shake Shack introduces a combo meal deal for the first time
data.description:
Shake Shack (NYSE:SHAK) has introduced its first-ever combo meal in a short test of the concept. The meal deal, which features a Chicken Shack sandwich, crinkle-cut fries, and a small soda, is priced at $9.99. The Chicken Shack features crispy chicken breast served over lettuce, pickles and a buttermilk-herb mayo on a toasted potato bun. 

The limited-time offer is available until March 15 at participating locations nationwide, excluding airports, stadiums, arenas, travel plazas, and museums. Customers must order through a kiosk in the restaurant, the Shack App, or the Shake Shack website, and enter the code "CHICKEN" at checkout to receive the offer. The offer is limited to one combo per order and cannot be combined with other promotions or add-ons like avocado, bacon, or cheese fries.

Of course, the promotion is part of a broader trend in the fast-food industry to create a value perception with customers. Shake Shack's (NYSE:SHAK) move follows similar new initiatives by competitors like McDonald's (MCD), Burger King (QSR), Taco Bell (YUM), Sonic, and Wendy's (WEN). Dine-in chain Applebee's (DIN) has also introduced a $9.99 meal deal, with options like the Big Cluckin' Chicken Sandwich or the Big Classic Bacon Cheeseburger.

During Shake Shack's (SHAK) recent earnings call, management said a strategic priority is driving comparable sales by increasing guest frequency. In 2024, the company generated same-Shack sales of 3.6%, and nearly flat traffic despite significant macro industry headwinds.

Shares of Shake Shack (SHAK) are down 28% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Array Technologies raised to Buy at Guggenheim as margin targets too conservative
data.description:
Array Technologies (NASDAQ:ARRY) +17.8% in Friday's trading, recouping all of the stock's YTD losses, as Guggenheim upgrades the solar tracking technology provider to Buy from Neutral with a $10 price target following the company's recent Q4 earnings report, which had prompted a quick 20% plunge.

Guggenheim's Joseph Osha says the Q4 report was better than it appeared at first glance, in part because the company could have done a more thorough job of clarifying the picture on 45X subsidies and debookings in Brazil, and the analyst says he now has more confidence in his 2025 and 2026 estimates, which are slightly above consensus for both years.

Osha says his analysis suggests Array Tech's (NASDAQ:ARRY) gross margin targets are conservative, specifically for H2 2025.

U.S. steel prices have climbed by a third since early January, and Osha believes Array Tech (ARRY) will benefit as the prices begin to flow through its financials; Array Tech does not earn higher margins on more expensive steel per se, but the higher revenue helps significantly with fixed cost absorption the analyst says, noting that steel comprises ~30% of the company's bill of materials.

Array (ARRY) says backlog with already-contracted steel is unusually long at this point, extending into Q3, so the higher steel pricing does not up in its financials until Q4; as it does, Osha believes the company's margins should improve, potentially more than the Street expects.

data.title: Apple says more personalized version of Siri is delayed
data.description:
• Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) said the updated, more personalized version of its digital voice assistant Siri has been delayed to 2026.
  • "It's going to take us longer than we thought to deliver on these features and we anticipated rolling them out in the coming year," the iPhone maker said in a statement obtained by Reuters.
  • No reason was given for the delay, but Seeking Alpha has previously reported that it has run into some engineering issues with the next version of its Siri assistant. The new Siri is reported to utilize more advanced large language models and could potentially allow for back-and-forth conversations and handle requests in a quicker manner.
  • Cupertino, Calif.-based Apple announced that a new version of its Siri assistant would be coming at its 2024 developer conference.
  • Apple shares rose 1.5% in midday trading on Friday.

data.title: Lumine Group reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Lumine Group press release (OTCPK:LMGIF): Q4 Revenue grew 31% to $187.1 million compared to $143.1 million in the same quarter prior year (including -9% organic growth after adjusting for foreign exchange impacts)
  • The Company generated net income of $29.4 million during the quarter, from net loss of $1,506.3 million in the same quarter prior year.

data.title: CDC reports 58 new measles cases as nationwide count surpasses 200
data.description:
• A weekly update from the CDC on the number of reported measles cases states that as of March 6, there are 222, 58 more than the prior week.
  • Most of those cases -- 99 (45%) -- are in individuals between 5 and 19 years old. Another 76 (34%) cases are in youngsters below the age of 5.
  • Of the 222 cases, 94% are either unvaccinated or vaccination status unknown.
  • A total of 38 cases (17%) are patients who are hospitalized.
  • Late Thursday, the New Mexico Department of Health disclosed a death linked to measles. That patient was unvaccinated.
  • Merck (NYSE:MRK) and GSK (NYSE:GSK) market the two MMR vaccines available in the U.S.

data.title: Atrium Mortgage Investment GAAP EPS of C$1.05, revenue of C$97.26M

  • Revenue of C$97.26M (-1.3% Y/Y).

data.title: Trump says he may impose tariffs on Canadian lumber, dairy products today
data.description:
President Donald Trump said Friday that he may impose reciprocal tariffs on imported labor and dairy products from Canada, possibly almost immediately. 

“They’ll be met with the exact same tariff unless they drop it, and that’s what reciprocal means,” Trump said in the Oval Office. “We may do it as early as today, or we’ll wait till Monday or Tuesday, but that’s what we’re going to do. We’re going to do, we’re going to charge.”

Trump on Thursday delayed tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports that are covered by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement trade pact that he negotiated with the neighbors during his first term. 

“They make it impossible for us to sell lumber or dairy products into Canada,” Trump said.

Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: White House policies' net effect is what matters, Federal Reserve's Jerome Powell says
data.description:
The new White House administration is making major changes in trade, immigration, fiscal policies, and deregulation. "It's the net effect of these policies" that matters, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Friday at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business.

It wouldn't be appropriate for the Fed to react to a one-time price spike from tariffs. "What really matter is long-term inflation expectations," he explained.

Overall, though, the U.S. economy remains healthy, he noted. The labor market is solid and inflation has been moving toward the central bank's 2% goal, he said.

"Many indicators show that the labor market is solid and broadly in balance," he said in prepared remarks.

The unemployment rate remains low near 4%. The labor market is not a source of wage inflation, Powell added.

In discussing the Fed's review of its monetary policy framework, Powell said he doesn't think the central bank needs to redefine price stability, which it defines as 2% inflation.
